univariate.age: Largest spike at age 0 — infant appointments brought by parents. Distribution spreads across 20-60 age range with gradual decline after 60. Working age adults visit clinics most. Very few patients above 80.
univariate.waiting_days: Heavily right skewed. Most appointments have 0-10 waiting days — patients seen quickly. Very few wait beyond 50 days. Long tail goes up to 179 days — those patients have highest chance of forgetting.
univariate.scheduled_hour: Peak booking hour is 7-8am. Most bookings happen between 7-11am then drop sharply after noon. Almost no bookings after 5pm. Clinic booking is essentially a morning activity.
univariate.day_of_week: No appointments on Saturday or Sunday — clinic closed weekends. Tuesday and Wednesday are busiest days. Thursday noticeably quieter. Friday has decent volume but higher no-show rates.
univariate.previous_noshow_rate: Massive spike at 0 — most patients are first-timers or have perfect attendance. Small groups at 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 are repeat no-show patients. Patients with rate 1.0 always miss — strongest predictor in the model.
univariate.gender: 65% female, 35% male. Women visit clinics more frequently — proactive about healthcare and bring children for checkups.
univariate.scholarship: Only 10% of patients are on Bolsa Familia welfare program. Small group but important — financially disadvantaged patients face transport and work barriers affecting attendance.
univariate.hypertension: 20% of patients have hypertension. 1 in 5 is significant — chronic condition patients need regular monitoring so they are more motivated to attend.
univariate.diabetes: 7% diabetic. Smaller than hypertension but meaningful — diabetic patients need frequent checkups so expected to show up more consistently.
univariate.alcoholism: Under 3% have alcoholism flagged. Very imbalanced column — limited individual predictive power but combined with other conditions still useful.
univariate.handicap: Almost everyone is 0. Levels 1-4 extremely rare — barely visible in chart. Grouped into binary has-disability flag during feature engineering.
univariate.sms_received: 32% received SMS reminder, 68% did not. Majority got no reminder at all. SMS recipients paradoxically had higher no-show rates — confirmed in bivariate analysis.
univariate.noshow: Clear 80-20 split — 88k showed up, 22k did not. This is the class imbalance problem. A naive model predicting everyone shows up gets 80% accuracy but is completely useless.
univariate.agegroup: Adult (36-55) largest group at 30k. Teen smallest at 6k. Child correctly shows 21k after fixing age bins — infant appointments included. Senior group reliable attenders.
univariate.hascondition: 75% have no condition, 25% have at least one chronic condition. Quarter of patients are medically significant — these patients show up more reliably.

num_num.age_vs_waitingdays: No relationship between age and waiting days. All ages wait roughly the same. Clinic does not prioritize slots by age — everyone waits equally regardless of how old they are.
num_num.age_vs_previousnoshowrate: Horizontal lines pattern because PreviousNoShowRate takes discrete values based on appointment history. Younger patients have more spread — more appointment history. Elderly cluster near 0 — consistent attenders.
num_num.waitingdays_vs_previousnoshowrate: Patients with high PreviousNoShowRate spread across ALL waiting day ranges — even same day appointments. Repeat no-show patients miss regardless of wait time. Their behavior is habitual not situational.

num_cat.age_by_noshow: Young adults 15-35 have slightly higher no-show proportion. Elderly 60+ orange proportion shrinks — older patients show up more consistently. Age alone is not a strong predictor but direction is clear.
num_cat.waitingdays_by_noshow: Most powerful chart. At 0 waiting days no-show is tiny. As days increase no-show grows. Patients waiting 25+ days have much higher no-show proportion. Confirms WaitingDays is a strong predictor.
num_cat.scheduledhour_by_noshow: No-show proportion consistent across all hours — no dramatic difference. Slight increase around hour 13-15. Booking hour is a weak predictor on its own.
num_cat.previousnoshowrate_by_noshow: Most powerful chart. At rate 0 showed-up dominates massively. At rate 1.0 almost entirely no-show. Patients with high past no-show history almost always miss again. Strongest single feature in the dataset.

cat_cat.gender_by_noshow: Orange proportion similar for both genders. Gender is not a strong predictor — male and female miss appointments at roughly the same rate.
cat_cat.scholarship_by_noshow: Scholarship patients have visibly higher no-show proportion. Welfare patients miss more — financial and transport barriers make attendance harder.
cat_cat.hypertension_by_noshow: Hypertensive patients have smaller no-show proportion. Chronic condition patients attend more — need regular medication and monitoring. Motivation is higher.
cat_cat.diabetes_by_noshow: Same pattern as hypertension. Diabetic patients show up more reliably. Serious illness means stronger reason to attend.
cat_cat.alcoholism_by_noshow: Small alcoholism group has slightly higher no-show proportion. Unpredictable lifestyle leads to more missed appointments.
cat_cat.sms_by_noshow: Patients who got SMS have higher no-show proportion. Counterintuitive — SMS did not help. Because SMS was sent to patients booked further ahead who were already higher risk.
cat_cat.hascondition_by_noshow: Patients with any condition have smaller no-show proportion. Combined medical condition flag confirms — sick patients show up more.
cat_cat.agegroup_by_noshow: YoungAdults most likely to skip — busy schedules, low health urgency. Seniors and Children most reliable — elderly take health seriously, children brought by parents.

bivariate.neighbourhood: Some neighbourhoods have 30%+ no-show rate vs 20% average. Geographic risk exists — certain areas consistently produce more missed appointments. Clinics in high-risk areas need more proactive outreach.
bivariate.waitbucket: Same day appointments — almost no no-shows. 1-7 days — no-show starts rising. 8-30 days — highest volume of no-shows in absolute numbers. 31+ days — worst no-show ratio. Longer the wait higher the risk.

multivariate.sms_by_waitbucket: Same day gets no SMS — no time to send reminder. As waiting days increase SMS proportion grows. But long-wait patients still miss despite getting SMS — reminder alone is not enough.
multivariate.scholarship_by_agegroup: YoungAdults have most scholarship patients. Seniors on welfare very rare. Financial hardship concentrated in working-age groups who face more competing priorities.

correlation: All 4 numeric features are independent of each other — no multicollinearity. Each feature brings unique information to the model. No column needs to be dropped due to redundancy. Good news for modeling.
